medications.indication_rationale: The patient is biochemically euthyroid; no thyroid hormone replacement is required. Starting thyroxine would be inappropriate and is explicitly not needed per the medication reconciliation.

A sedating anxiolytic should NOT be reflexively prescribed at a first telehealth contact for acute anxiety with throat symptoms: it can blunt airway protective reflexes and mask deterioration in a patient with a neck mass, carries dependence risk, and is unnecessary when non-drug de-escalation (paced breathing, hands off the neck, sips of cool water) breaks the panic cycle. Manage acutely with reassurance and behavioural techniques; defer any anxiolytic decision to the GP.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Stop pressing on and checking the neck; keep hands away from the throat.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Continuous self-palpation feeds the globus/panic cycle and can bruise the area; removing external pressure helps the throat muscles relax.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Practise slow, paced breathing (e.g. in for 4, out for 6) until the heart rate settles.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Counteracts hyperventilation and interrupts the panic response driving the choking sensation.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Take small, slow sips of cool water over about 10 minutes.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Swallowing cool fluid relaxes the cricopharyngeal/throat muscle tension and demonstrates that swallowing is intact.

patient_education_points.point: The lump you feel is muscle tension in the throat from anxiety, not the thyroid gland suddenly swelling — a goitre changes slowly over years, not minutes.
Your thyroid is slightly enlarged because of antibodies but it is working normally and is not blocking your breathing.
Keep your hands away from your neck so you do not bruise or irritate the area, and use slow breathing to settle the panic.
This assessment is by chat and cannot physically examine your neck — so watch for specific warning signs (below) and seek emergency care immediately if any appear.

follow_up_plan.interval: GP review within a couple of days; routine GP appointment within about 7 days if settling
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: resolution of the acute episode, ongoing anxiety and any panic pattern, examination of the goitre, and arranging a routine thyroid ultrasound and confirmation of thyroid function
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: any airway red flag — high-pitched whistling breathing (stridor), blue lips, sudden hoarseness, inability to swallow liquids, or a rapidly enlarging/painful neck — warrants immediate emergency care rather than waiting

must_include_items: Actively screen for airway red flags (stridor, cyanosis, inability to swallow liquids, voice change) before reassuring
Recognise the reassuring patient-obtainable features (full-sentence speech, no stridor, swallows water) as arguing strongly against airway obstruction
Provide in-the-moment de-escalation: stop palpating the neck, paced breathing, small sips of cool water
Communicate a clear, specific airway safety net with escalation to 000/ED for the red flags
Arrange prompt GP follow-up for the anxiety and thyroid review
acceptable_alternatives: Recommending a short period of telehealth re-contact or nurse-line follow-up within hours if the patient remains distressed but stable
Advising presentation to an urgent care clinic same-day if the patient cannot be reassured, as a cautious middle path short of ambulance dispatch
errors_of_omission: Reassuring without giving any airway safety-net advice
Failing to arrange any follow-up despite a known thyroid mass
Not screening for the danger signs before concluding it is benign
errors_of_commission: Dispatching a 000 ambulance or directing to ED for the current reassuring, patent-airway picture (over-triage)
Starting thyroid hormone replacement in a euthyroid patient
Stating with false certainty that the airway is definitively safe as though an examination had been performed
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass requires BOTH poles handled: appropriate de-escalation/reassurance (no unnecessary ambulance) AND an explicit airway safety net with follow-up. Over-triaging to 000/ED or dismissing without a safety net each fails the case.
